---
title: "Vector and Matrix Differentiation"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # generic vectors / points
GRAD   = "#c1121f"   # gradients / the answer direction
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # highlights / tangent moves
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0), ls="-"):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0, linestyle=ls), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center", zorder=z + 1)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This opens **Movement VI — Calculus on Vectors**, the
> last stretch of the Geometry-of-Data track, and it is the *one* place the track lifts
> calculus above the Calculus-I floor — done explicitly, from scratch. It assumes the **dot
> product** $a^\top x = \sum_i a_i x_i$ and the norm $\|x\|^2 = x^\top x$ ([GEOM-04]); the
> reading of a matrix as a **linear map** whose action $Ax$ is a linear combination of its
> columns ([GEOM-08]); and the **transpose** with its algebra $(AB)^\top = B^\top A^\top$ and
> the meaning of a **symmetric** matrix $A = A^\top$ ([GEOM-10]). From the floor it needs only
> single-variable differentiation — the power rule, the product rule, and the chain rule —
> which we restate as we use them. The one question: *what does it even mean to differentiate
> a function with respect to a whole vector (or matrix) of variables, and how do we keep the
> bookkeeping straight so the answers come out as clean objects we can compute with?* It sets
> up [GEOM-23] (the gradient as the direction of steepest ascent), [GEOM-24] (the Hessian as
> curvature), and [GEOM-25] (the gradient and Hessian of a quadratic form). It supplies the
> **multivariable critical-point condition** that Inference's score-equation work ([INFR-B2])
> stands on, and it builds the gradient object that every Optimization algorithm chases.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A machine-learning loss is a single number computed from a whole vector of parameters. To
train a model we must ask how that number changes as we nudge the parameters — but the
parameters are not one knob, they are dozens or millions. This article builds the calculus
of that question from the ground up. We define the **partial derivative** as "vary one
coordinate, freeze the rest," assemble the partials into the **gradient** vector and the
**Jacobian** matrix, fix a **layout convention** so the shapes always line up, and then
derive the handful of identities that do all the real work in the rest of the series:
$$
\nabla(a^\top x) = a, \qquad \nabla(x^\top x) = 2x, \qquad
\nabla(x^\top A x) = (A + A^\top)x, \qquad
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\, f(Ax) = A^\top \nabla f\big|_{Ax}.
$$
Every one of these is proved one component at a time, with nothing above Calc 1 assumed. The
single claim the article defends is that **vector differentiation is not a new kind of
calculus — it is ordinary single-variable calculus done coordinate-by-coordinate, with a
bookkeeping convention that lets us hide the indices and read the answer as a vector.**

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
To differentiate a scalar function $f$ of a vector $x = (x_1,\dots,x_n)$, differentiate it
**one coordinate at a time**, holding all other coordinates fixed — that is a *partial
derivative*, and it is pure Calc 1. Stack the $n$ partials into a vector and you have the
**gradient** $\nabla f$. The gradient is the vector that makes the first-order change in $f$
read as a single dot product:
$$
f(x + \Delta x) \approx f(x) + (\nabla f)^\top \Delta x .
$$
Everything else in this article — the layout conventions, the Jacobian, the chain rule — is
machinery for computing that vector cleanly without writing the indices out by hand.
:::

Hold onto the approximation in the box; it is the *why* behind every choice below. The whole
point of a derivative, since Calc 1, is **local linear approximation**: near a point, a
smooth function looks like a straight line, and the derivative is its slope. When the input
is a vector, "looks like a line" becomes "looks like a flat plane (a *linear* map)," and the
slope becomes a vector — the gradient — whose dot product with the nudge $\Delta x$ gives the
first-order change. If you ever lose the thread, return to that sentence: we are always
building the best linear approximation, just in more than one input dimension.

# Why we are doing this: the loss is a function of a vector {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view the entire way through: **why do we need to differentiate against a
whole vector at once, and why can't we just do it one variable at a time and forget about
it?** The answer is that the *answer itself* is a vector — a single object with a geometric
meaning — and that meaning is the engine of the rest of the series.

## The problem that forces it

Every supervised model in this series ends at the same place. You have parameters — a slope
and intercept in regression, a weight vector in a linear classifier, millions of weights in a
network — and you have a **loss**: one number measuring how badly the current parameters fit
the data. Collect the parameters into a vector $\theta = (\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)$. The loss
is then a function

$$
L : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}, \qquad \theta \mapsto L(\theta),
$$

that eats a whole vector of parameters and returns a single scalar. Training is the search for
the $\theta$ that makes $L$ smallest. From Calc 1 we know the way to find the bottom of a
function is to follow its slope downhill and stop where the slope is zero. But $L$ has no one
slope — it has a slope *in every direction* of the $n$-dimensional parameter space. The object
that packages all of those slopes into one is the gradient, and to find it we must learn to
differentiate with respect to a vector.

This is not a niche concern. The same need appeared the moment Legendre and Gauss wrote down
least squares in 1805 (the story of [REGR-01]): the sum of squared errors is a scalar function
of the *vector* of coefficients, and minimizing it meant setting a whole vector of partial
derivatives to zero. It is the reason the **normal equations** $X^\top X\hat\beta = X^\top y$
have the shape they do — that shape is what falls out when you differentiate $\|y - X\beta\|^2$
with respect to the vector $\beta$, a computation we set up here and finish in [GEOM-25]. The
notation we are about to build is the language the entire optimization half of data science is
written in.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Isn't this just multivariable calculus? Why dress it up as 'vector' differentiation?"* It
**is** multivariable calculus — there is no new theorem hiding here. What "vector
differentiation" adds is a *bookkeeping discipline*. Multivariable calculus, as usually taught,
leaves you holding a bag of $n$ separate partial derivatives, indexed by hand. Vector calculus
agrees to *stack* those partials into a vector or matrix and to fix a layout convention
(@sec-layout) so that the result is one clean object whose shape is predictable. That lets us
write $\nabla(x^\top A x) = (A+A^\top)x$ — a statement about whole vectors — instead of $n$
scalar equations. The payoff is that we can then *manipulate the answer algebraically*, the
same way the rest of the track manipulates matrices, instead of drowning in indices.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Picture the loss surface over a two-parameter model: a landscape of hills and valleys living
above the $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$ plane, with height $L(\theta_1,\theta_2)$. Stand at a point on
that surface. There is no single "slope" — which way are you even facing? Walk east (increase
$\theta_1$, hold $\theta_2$ fixed) and you measure one slope; that is $\partial L/\partial
\theta_1$. Walk north (increase $\theta_2$, hold $\theta_1$) and you measure another; that is
$\partial L/\partial \theta_2$. Those two numbers are the **partial derivatives**: the slope of
the surface along each coordinate axis. @fig-slices shows exactly this — two vertical slices
through a surface, each an ordinary Calc-1 curve whose slope is one partial derivative.

```{python}
#| label: fig-slices
#| fig-cap: 'A partial derivative is a Calc-1 slope along one axis. The surface is $f(x_1,x_2)=x_1^2+\\tfrac12 x_2^2$. Slicing it at fixed $x_2$ (red curve, varying $x_1$) gives a parabola whose slope is $\\partial f/\\partial x_1$; slicing at fixed $x_1$ (green curve, varying $x_2$) gives another whose slope is $\\partial f/\\partial x_2$. Differentiating with respect to a vector is nothing more than taking *both* of these ordinary derivatives and stacking them.'
def f(x1, x2):
    return x1**2 + 0.5 * x2**2

g = np.linspace(-2, 2, 60)
X1, X2 = np.meshgrid(g, g)
Z = f(X1, X2)

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6.6, 4.8))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")
ax.plot_surface(X1, X2, Z, cmap="viridis", alpha=0.55, linewidth=0, antialiased=True)

# point we slice at
p1, p2 = 1.0, 1.0
# slice varying x1 at fixed x2 = p2
t = np.linspace(-2, 2, 80)
ax.plot(t, np.full_like(t, p2), f(t, p2), color=GRAD, lw=3.2, label="vary $x_1$ (fix $x_2$)")
# slice varying x2 at fixed x1 = p1
ax.plot(np.full_like(t, p1), t, f(p1, t), color=ACCENT, lw=3.2, label="vary $x_2$ (fix $x_1$)")
ax.scatter([p1], [p2], [f(p1, p2)], color="black", s=40, depthshade=False)
ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$"); ax.set_zlabel("$f$")
ax.set_title("Each partial derivative is a slope along one axis")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9)
ax.view_init(elev=26, azim=-122)
plt.tight_layout()
```

Stacking those two slopes into a single arrow $(\partial L/\partial\theta_1,\,
\partial L/\partial\theta_2)$ gives a vector that lives in the *floor* of the landscape — the
parameter plane — and points in a definite compass direction. That arrow is the gradient. In
this article we earn the *vector*; the claim that it points uphill in the steepest direction is
the geometry we cash in next door in [GEOM-23]. For now the mental model is enough: **the
gradient is the arrow you get by measuring the slope along each axis and stacking those slopes
into one vector.**

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## From the Calc-1 derivative to the partial derivative {#sec-partial}

We start at the floor and build up, defining every symbol on first use. Recall the single
definition Calc 1 gives for the derivative of a one-variable function $h:\mathbb{R}\to
\mathbb{R}$ at a point $a$:

$$
h'(a) = \lim_{t \to 0} \frac{h(a + t) - h(a)}{t}.
$$ {#eq-calc1}

In words: nudge the input by a small amount $t$, see how much the output changed, divide by
the nudge, and take the nudge to zero. The result is the instantaneous rate of change — the
slope of the tangent line. That is the only calculus fact we will assume; everything else is
built from it.

Now let $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ be a scalar-valued function of a vector
$x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)^\top$. We cannot directly nudge "$x$" the way @eq-calc1 nudges a single
number, because $x$ has $n$ components and a nudge could point any of infinitely many ways. The
simplest, most disciplined choice is to nudge along **one coordinate axis at a time**.

::: {#def-partial}
**Definition (partial derivative).** The **partial derivative** of $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$
with respect to the $j$-th coordinate, at the point $x$, is
$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}(x)
= \lim_{t \to 0} \frac{f(x_1,\dots,x_j + t,\dots,x_n) - f(x_1,\dots,x_j,\dots,x_n)}{t},
$$ {#eq-partial-def}
provided the limit exists. Only the $j$-th coordinate is changed; the other $n-1$ coordinates
are held fixed.
:::

Compare @eq-partial-def with @eq-calc1: they are *the same limit*. If we define a one-variable
helper $h(t) = f(x_1,\dots,x_j + t,\dots,x_n)$ — the function you get by freezing every
coordinate except the $j$-th and letting that one vary — then @eq-partial-def is exactly
$h'(0)$. This is the crucial reduction, and it is worth saying as a slogan:

> **A partial derivative is an ordinary Calc-1 derivative of the function you get by treating
> every other variable as a constant.**

The symbol changes from $d$ to $\partial$ ("partial") for one reason only: to remind you that
other variables are present and being held still. The mechanics are identical to Calc 1. If
$f(x_1,x_2) = 3x_1^2 x_2 + 5x_2$, then to compute $\partial f/\partial x_1$ we pretend $x_2$ is
a fixed constant $c$: $f = 3c\,x_1^2 + 5c$, whose $x_1$-derivative is $6c\,x_1 = 6x_1 x_2$.
Likewise $\partial f/\partial x_2$ pretends $x_1$ is constant: $f = (3x_1^2)x_2 + 5x_2$, linear
in $x_2$, with derivative $3x_1^2 + 5$.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Does the existence of all the partial derivatives mean the function is differentiable?"* No —
and an interviewer asking this is probing whether you know the gap between partials and true
differentiability. Partials only test the function along the coordinate axes; a function can
have both partials at a point and still fail to have a coherent linear approximation there (the
standard counterexample is $f(x,y) = xy/(x^2+y^2)$ at the origin, which has both partials equal
to zero but is not even continuous). What *guarantees* differentiability is that the partials
exist **and are continuous** near the point — then the linear approximation in the Core box is
valid. Every function we differentiate in this track (polynomials, quadratic forms, smooth
losses) has continuous partials everywhere, so we are safely inside that regime; we name the
condition so you know it is there, not because it will bite us here.
:::

## The gradient: stacking the partials {#sec-gradient}

A function of $n$ variables has $n$ partial derivatives, one per coordinate. Carrying them
around as a loose list is exactly the bookkeeping mess we want to avoid. So we collect them
into a single vector.

::: {#def-gradient}
**Definition (gradient).** For $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ with all partials existing at $x$,
the **gradient** of $f$ at $x$ is the vector of partial derivatives
$$
\nabla f(x)
= \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \\[8pt]
\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \\[4pt]
\vdots \\[2pt]
\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}
\end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^n .
$$ {#eq-gradient-def}
The symbol $\nabla$ ("del" or "nabla") is read "gradient of." We also write
$\partial f/\partial x$ for the same object.
:::

The gradient lives in the **same space as $x$** — it is an $n$-vector, one slope per input
coordinate. That is the whole reason for stacking: the answer is now a geometric object in the
input space, an arrow we can draw, compare to other arrows, and feed into the dot-product
machinery the track has already built.

Why is this the *right* package and not an arbitrary one? Because it is precisely the vector
that makes the first-order Taylor approximation collapse into a single dot product, the
identity in the Core box. We prove that now, since it justifies everything.

::: {#thm-firstorder}
**Theorem (first-order approximation).** If $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ has continuous
partial derivatives near $x$, then for a small nudge $\Delta x = (\Delta x_1,\dots,\Delta
x_n)^\top$,
$$
f(x + \Delta x) = f(x) + \nabla f(x)^\top \, \Delta x + r(\Delta x),
$$
where the remainder $r(\Delta x)$ shrinks faster than $\|\Delta x\|$ (that is,
$r(\Delta x)/\|\Delta x\| \to 0$ as $\Delta x \to 0$).
:::

::: {.proof}
We change one coordinate at a time and add up the changes, applying the one-variable result at
each step. Define the intermediate points where we have already nudged the first $k$
coordinates:
$$
x^{(0)} = x, \qquad
x^{(k)} = x + (\Delta x_1, \dots, \Delta x_k, 0, \dots, 0)^\top .
$$
Then $x^{(n)} = x + \Delta x$, and the total change telescopes — each term cancels with the
next — into a sum of one-coordinate changes:
$$
f(x+\Delta x) - f(x)
= \sum_{k=1}^{n} \big[\, f(x^{(k)}) - f(x^{(k-1)}) \,\big].
$$
Going from $x^{(k-1)}$ to $x^{(k)}$ changes *only* the $k$-th coordinate, by $\Delta x_k$.
Holding the others fixed, $f$ is a one-variable function of that coordinate, so the
Calc-1 mean value theorem gives a point $\xi_k$ between $x_k$ and $x_k + \Delta x_k$ with
$$
f(x^{(k)}) - f(x^{(k-1)}) = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_k}(c_k)\,\Delta x_k,
$$
where $c_k$ is $x^{(k-1)}$ with its $k$-th coordinate replaced by $\xi_k$ — a point that lies
within $\|\Delta x\|$ of $x$. Summing,
$$
f(x+\Delta x) - f(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_k}(c_k)\,\Delta x_k .
$$
Now use continuity of the partials: as $\Delta x \to 0$ every $c_k \to x$, so
$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_k}(c_k) = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_k}(x) + \varepsilon_k$ with
$\varepsilon_k \to 0$. Substituting,
$$
f(x+\Delta x) - f(x)
= \underbrace{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_k}(x)\,\Delta x_k}_{=\ \nabla f(x)^\top \Delta x}
\;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \varepsilon_k\,\Delta x_k}_{=\ r(\Delta x)} .
$$
The first sum is exactly the dot product $\nabla f(x)^\top \Delta x$ (definition of the dot
product, [GEOM-04]). For the remainder, $|r(\Delta x)| \le \big(\sum_k |\varepsilon_k|\big)
\max_k|\Delta x_k| \le \big(\sum_k |\varepsilon_k|\big)\|\Delta x\|$, so
$|r(\Delta x)|/\|\Delta x\| \le \sum_k|\varepsilon_k| \to 0$. That is the claim. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This theorem is the spine of the article. It says the gradient *is* the local linear map: nudge
$x$ by $\Delta x$ and, to first order, $f$ changes by $\nabla f^\top \Delta x$. Read against the
Calc-1 picture, $\nabla f$ plays the exact role $h'(a)$ played in $h(a+t)\approx h(a)+h'(a)t$ —
the slope, generalized to a vector. The "stacking" of @def-gradient is forced: it is the only
arrangement of the partials for which $\nabla f^\top \Delta x$ recovers the change in $f$.

## A worked micro-derivation, indices visible {#sec-micro}

Before we hide the indices behind clean identities, let us do one gradient *completely by
hand*, every index on the page, so the later shortcuts are never mysterious. Take

$$
f(x) = x_1^2 + x_1 x_2 + 4 x_2 , \qquad x = (x_1, x_2)^\top \in \mathbb{R}^2 .
$$

Compute each partial by freezing the other variable and using ordinary Calc-1 rules. For
$\partial f/\partial x_1$, treat $x_2$ as a constant: the term $x_1^2$ differentiates to $2x_1$
(power rule), $x_1 x_2$ has constant coefficient $x_2$ so it differentiates to $x_2$, and
$4x_2$ is constant in $x_1$ so it differentiates to $0$. Hence $\partial f/\partial x_1 = 2x_1
+ x_2$. For $\partial f/\partial x_2$, freeze $x_1$: $x_1^2$ is constant ($\to 0$), $x_1 x_2$
has coefficient $x_1$ ($\to x_1$), and $4x_2$ ($\to 4$). Hence $\partial f/\partial x_2 = x_1 +
4$. Stacking,

$$
\nabla f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 2x_1 + x_2 \\ x_1 + 4 \end{bmatrix} .
$$

That is the entire mechanism. Everything from here on is repeating this move on functions
written in vector notation and then *recognizing the stacked answer as a vector expression*, so
we never have to write the indices again.

## Layout convention: why shapes line up {#sec-layout}

Here is the one genuine subtlety in the whole subject, and the source of every sign-and-shape
headache people meet later. When we differentiate, we must decide *how to arrange the answer*.
The gradient of a scalar with respect to a column vector $x$ — should it be a column or a row?
Different textbooks choose differently, and mixing the two is the number-one source of
transpose errors.

We adopt, and state plainly, the **denominator layout** (also called the *gradient
convention*), which is the standard in optimization and machine learning:

::: {.callout-important title="The layout convention we use"}
The derivative of a **scalar** $f$ with respect to a **column vector** $x\in\mathbb{R}^n$ is a
**column vector** of the same shape as $x$:
$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = \nabla f =
\Big[\tfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \dots, \tfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}\Big]^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times 1}.
$$
The derivative of a **vector** $g\in\mathbb{R}^m$ with respect to a **column vector**
$x\in\mathbb{R}^n$ is the **Jacobian**, an $m\times n$ matrix whose $(i,j)$ entry is $\partial
g_i/\partial x_j$ (@sec-jacobian).
:::

The reason to fix the gradient as a column "same shape as $x$" is purely practical, and it ties
straight back to @thm-firstorder: with this choice the first-order change is the dot product
$\nabla f^\top \Delta x$ (a row times a column, giving a scalar), and a gradient-descent step
$x \gets x - \eta\,\nabla f$ subtracts a vector of the same shape as $x$ — both expressions
type-check on sight. We will hold this convention rigidly for the rest of the track.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The single most common bug in vector calculus is a transpose introduced by silently switching
layout conventions mid-derivation.** If you ever find yourself needing to "add a transpose to
make the shapes work," stop: you have almost certainly mixed denominator layout (gradient is a
column) with numerator layout (gradient is a row). Pick one — we use denominator layout
throughout — and never switch inside a derivation. When in doubt, drop to indices as in
@sec-micro and the correct shape is forced, with no convention to get wrong.
:::

## The core identities, derived from scratch {#sec-identities}

We now derive the handful of vector-derivative facts the rest of the series leans on. Each is
proved by computing one component with @def-partial — ordinary Calc 1 — and then recognizing the
stacked result. No identity is asserted; each is built.

### The linear form: $\nabla(a^\top x) = a$ {#sec-linear}

Let $a = (a_1,\dots,a_n)^\top$ be a fixed constant vector and define the scalar function
$f(x) = a^\top x = \sum_{i=1}^n a_i x_i$ — the dot product of the constant $a$ with the variable
$x$ ([GEOM-04]). Differentiate with respect to the $j$-th coordinate. In the sum $\sum_i a_i
x_i$, the only term containing $x_j$ is $a_j x_j$; every other term is constant in $x_j$ and
contributes $0$. The surviving term is linear in $x_j$ with coefficient $a_j$, so

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\,(a^\top x)
= \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\sum_{i=1}^n a_i x_i
= a_j .
$$

The $j$-th partial is $a_j$, for every $j$. Stacking the partials (@def-gradient) gives the
vector whose $j$-th entry is $a_j$ — which is $a$ itself:

$$
\boxed{\;\nabla(a^\top x) = a.\;}
$$ {#eq-grad-linear}

This is the vector analogue of the Calc-1 fact $\frac{d}{dx}(ax) = a$: the derivative of a
linear function is its constant coefficient. Note that $x^\top a = a^\top x$ (a dot product is
symmetric, [GEOM-04]), so $\nabla(x^\top a) = a$ as well — order inside the dot product does not
matter here.

### The squared norm: $\nabla(x^\top x) = 2x$ {#sec-normsq}

Now $f(x) = x^\top x = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2 = \|x\|^2$, the squared length of $x$ ([GEOM-04]).
Differentiate with respect to $x_j$. In $\sum_i x_i^2$, the only term containing $x_j$ is
$x_j^2$; all others are constant in $x_j$. By the power rule, $\frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}
x_j^2 = 2x_j$, so

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\,(x^\top x) = 2x_j .
$$

The $j$-th partial is $2x_j$; stacking gives the vector with $j$-th entry $2x_j$, which is $2x$:

$$
\boxed{\;\nabla(x^\top x) = 2x.\;}
$$ {#eq-grad-normsq}

The vector analogue of $\frac{d}{dx}x^2 = 2x$ — the same factor of two, the same shape of
answer, now a vector. @fig-gradients draws both @eq-grad-linear and @eq-grad-normsq as gradient
fields so the algebra has a picture: the gradient of a linear form is one constant arrow
everywhere, and the gradient of $\|x\|^2$ points radially outward, growing with distance from
the origin.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gradients
#| fig-cap: 'The two foundational gradients, drawn as vector fields with their level sets behind them. **Left:** $f(x)=a^\\top x$ with $a=(1,2)$. Its level sets are parallel lines; its gradient $\\nabla f = a$ is the *same* constant arrow at every point, pointing perpendicular to the level lines toward higher $f$. **Right:** $f(x)=\\|x\\|^2$. Its level sets are circles; its gradient $\\nabla f = 2x$ points straight out from the origin and lengthens as $\\|x\\|$ grows. (That gradients sit perpendicular to level sets and point uphill is the geometry proved next door in GEOM-23; here we have only computed the vectors.)'
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.2))

# Left: linear form a^T x
a = np.array([1.0, 2.0])
g = np.linspace(-2.5, 2.5, 9)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(g, g)
cg = np.linspace(-2.6, 2.6, 200)
CX, CY = np.meshgrid(cg, cg)
axL.contour(CX, CY, a[0]*CX + a[1]*CY, levels=10, colors=PALE, linewidths=1.0)
axL.quiver(GX, GY, np.full_like(GX, a[0]), np.full_like(GY, a[1]),
           color=GRAD, angles="xy", scale_units="xy", scale=6, width=0.006)
axL.set_title(r"$f(x)=a^\top x,\ \ \nabla f = a$ (constant)")
axL.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$x_2$"); axL.set_aspect("equal")
axL.set_xlim(-2.6, 2.6); axL.set_ylim(-2.6, 2.6)

# Right: squared norm
axR.contour(CX, CY, CX**2 + CY**2, levels=np.arange(1, 14, 2), colors=PALE, linewidths=1.0)
axR.quiver(GX, GY, 2*GX, 2*GY, color=GRAD, angles="xy",
           scale_units="xy", scale=10, width=0.006)
axR.set_title(r"$f(x)=\|x\|^2,\ \ \nabla f = 2x$ (radial)")
axR.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axR.set_ylabel("$x_2$"); axR.set_aspect("equal")
axR.set_xlim(-2.6, 2.6); axR.set_ylim(-2.6, 2.6)
plt.tight_layout()
```

### The quadratic form: $\nabla(x^\top A x) = (A + A^\top)x$ {#sec-quadform}

This is the workhorse — the one that powers the normal equations, the Hessian story, and the
quadratic-form article [GEOM-25]. Let $A$ be a fixed $n\times n$ matrix with entries $A_{ik}$,
and define the scalar **quadratic form** (the object [GEOM-17] studies geometrically)

$$
f(x) = x^\top A x = \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{k=1}^n A_{ik}\, x_i x_k .
$$ {#eq-quad}

To get @eq-quad in index form, note $Ax$ has $i$-th entry $\sum_k A_{ik}x_k$, and $x^\top(Ax) =
\sum_i x_i \sum_k A_{ik}x_k$, which is the stated double sum. Now differentiate with respect to
$x_j$. A term $A_{ik}x_i x_k$ depends on $x_j$ only when $i = j$, or $k = j$, or both. We split
the double sum into exactly those cases — careful bookkeeping, no shortcuts.

**Case $i = j$, $k \neq j$.** The term is $A_{jk}x_j x_k$ with $x_k$ a constant in $x_j$;
$\partial/\partial x_j$ gives $A_{jk}x_k$. Summing over $k\neq j$: $\sum_{k\neq j}A_{jk}x_k$.

**Case $k = j$, $i \neq j$.** The term is $A_{ij}x_i x_j$ with $x_i$ constant in $x_j$;
$\partial/\partial x_j$ gives $A_{ij}x_i$. Summing over $i\neq j$: $\sum_{i\neq j}A_{ij}x_i$.

**Case $i = j$ and $k = j$.** The single term is $A_{jj}x_j^2$; by the power rule
$\partial/\partial x_j$ gives $2A_{jj}x_j$.

All other terms (with $i\neq j$ and $k\neq j$) are constant in $x_j$ and contribute $0$. Adding
the three surviving pieces,

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}
= \sum_{k\neq j}A_{jk}x_k \;+\; \sum_{i\neq j}A_{ij}x_i \;+\; 2A_{jj}x_j .
$$

Now fold the lone $2A_{jj}x_j$ back into the two sums — split it as $A_{jj}x_j + A_{jj}x_j$ and
hand one copy to each sum, which lets each sum run over *all* indices including the previously
excluded $j$:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}
= \underbrace{\sum_{k=1}^n A_{jk}x_k}_{j\text{-th entry of } Ax}
\;+\;
\underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n A_{ij}x_i}_{j\text{-th entry of } A^\top x} .
$$

Identify each sum. The first, $\sum_k A_{jk}x_k$, is the $j$-th entry of the vector $Ax$ (a row
of $A$ dotted with $x$, [GEOM-08]). The second, $\sum_i A_{ij}x_i$, sums down the $j$-th *column*
of $A$ against $x$ — and summing down the $j$-th column of $A$ is the same as summing across the
$j$-th *row* of $A^\top$, since $(A^\top)_{ji} = A_{ij}$ ([GEOM-10]). So it is the $j$-th entry
of $A^\top x$. The $j$-th partial is therefore the $j$-th entry of $Ax + A^\top x$, for every
$j$. Stacking over all $j$:

$$
\boxed{\;\nabla(x^\top A x) = Ax + A^\top x = (A + A^\top)\,x.\;}
$$ {#eq-grad-quad}

Two special cases are worth pinning down, because they are the ones that actually appear.

**Symmetric $A$.** If $A = A^\top$ (a symmetric matrix, [GEOM-10] — and covariance matrices,
Gram matrices $B^\top B$, and Hessians are all symmetric, [GEOM-16]), then $A + A^\top = 2A$ and

$$
\nabla(x^\top A x) = 2Ax \qquad (A = A^\top).
$$ {#eq-grad-quad-sym}

This is the clean, memorable form, and the exact vector analogue of $\frac{d}{dx}(ax^2) = 2ax$.

**Recovering @eq-grad-normsq.** Take $A = I$, the identity matrix. Then $x^\top I x = x^\top x$
and @eq-grad-quad-sym gives $\nabla(x^\top x) = 2Ix = 2x$ — exactly @eq-grad-normsq. The
quadratic identity contains the squared-norm identity as the special case $A=I$, which is a good
consistency check that we have not slipped a factor.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why does the answer involve $A + A^\top$ and not just $A$? People always write $2Ax$."* Because
the general quadratic form does not "see" the asymmetric part of $A$ symmetrically. The clean
$2Ax$ is correct *only when $A$ is symmetric*. For a general $A$, write $A = S + K$ with $S =
\tfrac12(A+A^\top)$ symmetric and $K = \tfrac12(A - A^\top)$ antisymmetric ($K^\top = -K$). The
antisymmetric part contributes nothing to the value. To see it, use that $x^\top K x$ is a
$1\times 1$ matrix — a scalar — and a scalar equals its own transpose ($s^\top = s$ when $s$ is
$1\times 1$). So $x^\top K x = (x^\top K x)^\top$, and the transpose-of-a-product rule $(ABC)^\top
= C^\top B^\top A^\top$ ([GEOM-10], applied with $(x^\top)^\top = x$) turns this into $(x^\top K
x)^\top = x^\top K^\top x = x^\top(-K)x = -\,x^\top K x$. A number equal to its own negative must
be $0$, so $x^\top K x = 0$ for all $x$. Thus $x^\top A x = x^\top S x$ — the
form only depends on the symmetric part $S$ — and @eq-grad-quad gives $\nabla = (A+A^\top)x = 2Sx$,
consistent with applying @eq-grad-quad-sym to $S$. The upshot: *you may always symmetrize $A$
before differentiating*, and in practice (covariance, $X^\top X$, Hessians) $A$ is already
symmetric, so $2Ax$ is what you use. The $A+A^\top$ form is the honest general statement that
collapses to it.
:::

## The Jacobian: differentiating a vector-valued function {#sec-jacobian}

So far the output of $f$ has been a single number. Sometimes the output is itself a vector — for
instance $g(x) = Ax$ maps $\mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$. Differentiating a vector with respect
to a vector produces a *matrix* of all the partials, called the Jacobian.

::: {#def-jacobian}
**Definition (Jacobian).** For $g:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$, $g(x) = (g_1(x),\dots,g_m(x))^\top$,
the **Jacobian** at $x$ is the $m\times n$ matrix
$$
J_g(x) = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}
= \begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{\partial g_1}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial g_1}{\partial x_n} \\[8pt]
\vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[4pt]
\dfrac{\partial g_m}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial g_m}{\partial x_n}
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad (J_g)_{ij} = \frac{\partial g_i}{\partial x_j} .
$$ {#eq-jacobian-def}
Row $i$ holds the gradient (transposed) of the $i$-th output component; column $j$ holds the
effect of nudging the $j$-th input on every output.
:::

The Jacobian is the honest multi-output generalization of @thm-firstorder: it is the matrix
that makes the first-order approximation read $g(x + \Delta x) \approx g(x) + J_g(x)\,\Delta x$
— a matrix times a nudge, returning a nudge in output space. When $m = 1$ the Jacobian is a
single row, the transpose of the gradient; the gradient is a column by our convention
(@sec-layout), so $\nabla f = (J_f)^\top$ in that case. Keeping the gradient a column and the
Jacobian "outputs-by-inputs" is what makes the chain rule below come out without stray
transposes.

The cleanest Jacobian is the one we need most: the linear map $g(x) = Ax$.

::: {#thm-jac-linear}
**Theorem (Jacobian of a linear map).** If $g(x) = Ax$ for a constant $m\times n$ matrix $A$,
then $J_g(x) = A$ at every $x$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The $i$-th output component is $g_i(x) = \sum_{k=1}^n A_{ik}x_k$ ([GEOM-08]). Differentiate with
respect to $x_j$: the only term containing $x_j$ is $A_{ij}x_j$, linear with coefficient
$A_{ij}$, so $\partial g_i/\partial x_j = A_{ij}$. Therefore $(J_g)_{ij} = A_{ij}$ for all
$i,j$, i.e. $J_g = A$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the vector analogue of $\frac{d}{dx}(ax) = a$ once more — the derivative of a linear map
*is* the map's matrix. It is the single fact we feed into the chain rule next.

## The chain rule for $f(Ax)$ {#sec-chain}

The last tool, and the one optimization uses constantly: differentiating a scalar function
composed with a linear map. Let $f:\mathbb{R}^m\to\mathbb{R}$ be smooth, let $A$ be a constant
$m\times n$ matrix, and define the composite scalar function

$$
F(x) = f(Ax), \qquad F:\mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}.
$$

We want $\nabla F(x)$, an $n$-vector. We will first recall the Calc-1 chain rule, then state and
prove its multivariable form, then read off the answer.

The Calc-1 chain rule says $\frac{d}{dt}\,h(u(t)) = h'(u(t))\,u'(t)$ — "derivative of the
outside, evaluated at the inside, times the derivative of the inside." The multivariable
statement we need is its faithful generalization:

::: {#thm-chain}
**Theorem (chain rule).** Let $u:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$ be differentiable with Jacobian
$J_u(x)$, and let $f:\mathbb{R}^m\to\mathbb{R}$ be differentiable with gradient $\nabla f$. Then
$F(x) = f(u(x))$ has gradient
$$
\nabla F(x) = J_u(x)^\top \, \nabla f\big(u(x)\big) .
$$ {#eq-chain}
:::

::: {.proof}
We compute the $j$-th partial of $F$ from @def-partial and recognize the result. Write $u =
(u_1,\dots,u_m)$, and fix the coordinate index $j$. The $j$-th partial of $F$ is, by definition,
$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial x_j}(x)
= \lim_{t\to 0}\frac{F(x + t e_j) - F(x)}{t}
= \lim_{t\to 0}\frac{f\big(u(x + t e_j)\big) - f\big(u(x)\big)}{t},
$$
where $e_j$ is the vector with a $1$ in position $j$ and $0$ elsewhere — so $x + t e_j$ nudges
*only* the $j$-th input. We do not assume any multivariable chain rule; we build this one from the
first-order approximation @thm-firstorder, which we have already proved.

**Expand the inner nudge.** Set $u^0 = u(x)$. As $t\to 0$ the inner point moves by
$$
\Delta u(t) \;=\; u(x + t e_j) - u(x),
$$
an $m$-vector whose $i$-th component is $u_i(x + t e_j) - u_i(x)$. Each $u_i$ is an ordinary
scalar function of the single varying coordinate $x_j$ here (every other coordinate is held
fixed), so by the very definition of the partial derivative @def-partial,
$$
\Delta u_i(t) = \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j}(x)\, t + o(t),
$$
where $o(t)$ denotes a term with $o(t)/t \to 0$ as $t\to 0$ (the standard "smaller than linear"
remainder; it is exactly $\big[\tfrac{\Delta u_i(t)}{t} - \tfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j}(x)\big]t$,
which the limit in @def-partial sends to zero faster than $t$). In particular $\Delta u(t)\to 0$ as
$t\to 0$, and $\|\Delta u(t)\| = O(t)$.

**Apply the first-order approximation to the outer function.** Since $f$ has continuous partials,
@thm-firstorder applied at the point $u^0$ with nudge $\Delta u(t)$ gives
$$
f\big(u(x + t e_j)\big) - f\big(u(x)\big)
= f(u^0 + \Delta u) - f(u^0)
= \nabla f(u^0)^\top \Delta u(t) + r\big(\Delta u(t)\big),
$$
with $r(\Delta u)/\|\Delta u\| \to 0$. Substitute the component expansion of $\Delta u$:
$$
f\big(u(x + t e_j)\big) - f\big(u(x)\big)
= \sum_{i=1}^m \frac{\partial f}{\partial u_i}(u^0)\Big[\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j}(x)\,t + o(t)\Big]
  \;+\; r\big(\Delta u(t)\big).
$$
**Divide by $t$ and take the limit.** Dividing by $t$,
$$
\frac{f(u(x+te_j)) - f(u(x))}{t}
= \sum_{i=1}^m \frac{\partial f}{\partial u_i}(u^0)\,\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j}(x)
  \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^m \frac{\partial f}{\partial u_i}(u^0)\frac{o(t)}{t}}_{\to\,0}
  \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{r(\Delta u(t))}{t}}_{\to\,0}.
$$
The first error term vanishes because each $o(t)/t\to 0$. The second vanishes because $\|\Delta
u(t)\| = O(t)$, so $\big|r(\Delta u)/t\big| = \big(|r(\Delta u)|/\|\Delta u\|\big)\,(\|\Delta
u\|/t) \to 0\cdot O(1) = 0$. Taking $t\to 0$ leaves exactly the multivariable chain rule for one
coordinate,
$$
\frac{\partial F}{\partial x_j}
= \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial f}{\partial u_i}\big(u(x)\big)\,\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j}(x) .
$$ {#eq-chain-coord}
Now recognize the right-hand side as a dot product: it is the dot product of the vector $\big(\partial
f/\partial u_i\big)_i = \nabla f$ with the vector $\big(\partial u_i/\partial x_j\big)_i$, which
is the $j$-th *column* of the Jacobian $J_u$ (the $j$-th column collects $\partial u_i/\partial
x_j$ over all output indices $i$, by @eq-jacobian-def). The dot product of $\nabla f$ with the
$j$-th column of $J_u$ is exactly the $j$-th entry of $J_u^\top \nabla f$ (multiplying by
$J_u^\top$ turns columns of $J_u$ into rows that dot against $\nabla f$, [GEOM-10]). So the
$j$-th partial of $F$ is the $j$-th entry of $J_u^\top \nabla f$, for every $j$; stacking gives
$\nabla F = J_u^\top \nabla f$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The transpose on $J_u$ is *not* a fudge — it is forced by our denominator-layout convention
(@sec-layout) and is the visible reason we fixed that convention. The gradient $\nabla f$ lives
in the $m$-dimensional output space; $J_u^\top$ is the $n\times m$ matrix that carries it back to
the $n$-dimensional input space, which is exactly where a gradient of $F$ must live. Shapes line
up: $(n\times m)(m\times 1) = (n\times 1)$.

Now specialize to the case we actually need, $u(x) = Ax$. By @thm-jac-linear the Jacobian is
$J_u = A$, constant, so @eq-chain becomes

$$
\boxed{\;\nabla_x\, f(Ax) = A^\top\, \nabla f\big|_{Ax}.\;}
$$ {#eq-chain-linear}

where $\nabla f|_{Ax}$ means "the gradient of $f$, evaluated at the point $Ax$." The constant
matrix $A$ comes out transposed. This single identity is the heart of backpropagation through a
linear layer and the engine of the least-squares gradient we assemble next.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Differentiate the least-squares loss $L(\beta) = \|y - X\beta\|^2$ with respect to $\beta$."*
This is the canonical interview question, and @eq-chain-linear plus the squared-norm identity
answer it in two lines — let us do it, since it is the bridge to the rest of the series. Write
the residual as the vector $r(\beta) = y - X\beta$, so $L(\beta) = r^\top r = \|r\|^2$, a
composite: square-the-norm $\circ$ the affine map $\beta \mapsto y - X\beta$. The outer function
is $f(r) = r^\top r$ with $\nabla_r f = 2r$ (@eq-grad-normsq). The inner map $\beta \mapsto y -
X\beta$ is affine; its Jacobian with respect to $\beta$ is $-X$ (the constant $y$ has zero
derivative; the linear part $-X\beta$ contributes $-X$ by @thm-jac-linear). Apply the chain rule
@eq-chain:
$$
\nabla_\beta L = (-X)^\top\,(2r) = -2X^\top (y - X\beta) = 2\big(X^\top X\,\beta - X^\top y\big).
$$
Setting $\nabla_\beta L = 0$ gives $X^\top X\,\beta = X^\top y$ — the **normal equations**. That
is precisely the multiple-regression result [REGR-05] needs and the quadratic-form minimization
[GEOM-25] finishes; the entire least-squares estimator falls out of the three boxed identities
of this article. If you can produce these two lines on a whiteboard, you have shown command of
exactly what this article exists to teach.
:::

## Differentiating with respect to a matrix {#sec-matrix-deriv}

The same stacking idea handles a scalar that depends on a whole *matrix* of variables. If
$f:\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}\to\mathbb{R}$ takes a matrix $X$ to a number, its derivative is the
matrix of partials, laid out in the same shape as $X$ (denominator layout again):

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}, \qquad
\Big(\frac{\partial f}{\partial X}\Big)_{ij} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial X_{ij}} .
$$ {#eq-matrix-deriv}

There is no new idea here — it is @def-gradient with the partials arranged in a grid instead of
a column. Two cases recur often enough to record, and we derive the first to show the mechanics
are unchanged.

::: {#thm-trace}
**Theorem (gradient of a linear trace form).** For constant matrices, $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial
X}\,\mathrm{tr}(A^\top X) = A$, where $\mathrm{tr}$ is the trace (sum of diagonal entries).
:::

::: {.proof}
Recall the trace identity $\mathrm{tr}(A^\top X) = \sum_{i}\sum_{k}(A^\top)_{ik}X_{ki} =
\sum_i\sum_k A_{ki}X_{ki}$ — the elementwise sum of $A$ against $X$ (this is the matrix dot
product). Differentiate with respect to the single entry $X_{pq}$: the only term containing
$X_{pq}$ is $A_{pq}X_{pq}$, with coefficient $A_{pq}$, so $\partial/\partial X_{pq} = A_{pq}$.
The $(p,q)$ entry of the answer is $A_{pq}$ for every $p,q$, i.e. the derivative matrix *is* $A$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The second, used in covariance and Gaussian-likelihood derivations and recorded here for
completeness, is $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial X}\log\det X = (X^{-1})^\top$ for invertible $X$ — its
derivation needs the determinant expansion of [GEOM-13] and we point there rather than reproduce
it, since this article's job is the vector case and the matrix case is the same stacking
principle applied to a grid.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the identities on honest problems, and verify each gradient *numerically* against the
analytic formula — the one place code earns its keep here, by confirming the hand-derivation
against a finite-difference check (the discrete shadow of @def-partial).

## Example 1 — gradient of a least-squares loss on real-shaped data {#sec-ex-ls}

Take a small regression design: $n = 6$ observations, $p = 3$ features (an intercept column plus
two predictors). The loss is $L(\beta) = \|y - X\beta\|^2$ and its gradient, by the interview
derivation above, is $\nabla_\beta L = 2(X^\top X\beta - X^\top y)$. We compute both the
formula and a finite-difference approximation and check they agree.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-ls-gradient
#| echo: true
import numpy as np
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# A small, concrete design matrix: intercept + 2 predictors, 6 observations.
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(6), rng.normal(size=6), rng.normal(size=6)])
beta_true = np.array([1.5, -2.0, 0.7])
y = X @ beta_true + rng.normal(scale=0.3, size=6)   # noisy outcome

beta = np.array([0.0, 0.0, 0.0])                    # a candidate parameter vector

def loss(b):
    r = y - X @ b
    return r @ r                                    # ||y - Xb||^2 = r^T r

# Analytic gradient from the boxed identities: 2 (X^T X b - X^T y).
grad_analytic = 2 * (X.T @ X @ beta - X.T @ y)

# Finite-difference gradient: the discrete shadow of the partial-derivative limit.
def finite_diff_grad(fn, b, h=1e-6):
    g = np.zeros_like(b)
    for j in range(len(b)):
        step = np.zeros_like(b); step[j] = h
        g[j] = (fn(b + step) - fn(b - step)) / (2 * h)   # central difference
    return g

grad_numeric = finite_diff_grad(loss, beta)
print("analytic gradient 2(X^T X b - X^T y):", np.round(grad_analytic, 6))
print("finite-difference gradient          :", np.round(grad_numeric, 6))
print("max abs difference                  :", np.max(np.abs(grad_analytic - grad_numeric)))
```

The two gradients agree to the precision of the finite-difference step. This is the operational
content of @sec-chain: the hand-derived $2(X^\top X\beta - X^\top y)$ is not a formula to trust on
faith — it matches what you get by literally perturbing each coordinate and measuring the change,
which is @def-partial made discrete. @fig-ls-descent shows the gradient as an arrow on the loss
surface over a two-parameter slice, pointing the way the loss climbs.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ls-descent
#| fig-cap: 'The least-squares gradient as an arrow on the loss surface. Fixing the intercept at its optimum and varying the two slope parameters, the contours show $L(\\beta)$ and the red arrow is $\\nabla_\\beta L$ at a sample point — it points directly *uphill*, perpendicular to the contour through that point. Its negative (the descent direction) is what an optimizer steps along. We computed this vector entirely from the boxed identities; GEOM-23 proves it is the steepest-ascent direction, and Optimization turns the descent step into an algorithm.'
# Solve for the optimal intercept, then make a 2D loss slice over the two slopes.
beta_hat = np.linalg.solve(X.T @ X, X.T @ y)
b1g = np.linspace(beta_hat[1] - 2.5, beta_hat[1] + 2.5, 80)
b2g = np.linspace(beta_hat[2] - 2.5, beta_hat[2] + 2.5, 80)
B1, B2 = np.meshgrid(b1g, b2g)
Lsurf = np.zeros_like(B1)
for i in range(B1.shape[0]):
    for j in range(B1.shape[1]):
        b = np.array([beta_hat[0], B1[i, j], B2[i, j]])
        r = y - X @ b
        Lsurf[i, j] = r @ r

# pick a sample point and its analytic gradient (the two slope components)
bs = np.array([beta_hat[0], beta_hat[1] + 1.6, beta_hat[2] - 1.3])
g_full = 2 * (X.T @ X @ bs - X.T @ y)
g2 = g_full[1:]                                   # gradient in the (b1, b2) slice
g2u = g2 / np.linalg.norm(g2) * 1.2               # scaled for drawing

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.0, 4.8))
cs = ax.contour(B1, B2, Lsurf, levels=14, colors=PALE, linewidths=1.0)
ax.scatter([beta_hat[1]], [beta_hat[2]], color=ACCENT, s=80, zorder=5,
           edgecolor="white", label=r"minimum $\hat\beta$")
ax.scatter([bs[1]], [bs[2]], color="black", s=40, zorder=5)
draw_vec(ax, (bs[1], bs[2]), (bs[1] + g2u[0], bs[2] + g2u[1]), color=GRAD,
         label=r"$\nabla L$", label_offset=(0.25, 0.2))
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\beta_1$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$\beta_2$")
ax.set_title(r"$\nabla_\beta L$ points uphill, $\perp$ to the contour")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
ax.set_aspect("equal")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 2 — gradient of a quadratic form with a genuinely asymmetric matrix {#sec-ex-quad}

Now the edge-of-applicability case, chosen to expose the trap people fall into: a quadratic form
$x^\top A x$ with $A$ **not symmetric**. If you reflexively write $\nabla = 2Ax$ you get the
wrong answer; the correct one is $(A + A^\top)x$ from @eq-grad-quad. We verify the honest formula
and show the naive one fails.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-asym-quad
#| echo: true
import numpy as np
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)

A = np.array([[2.0, 5.0, -1.0],     # deliberately NOT symmetric (A != A^T)
              [0.0, 3.0,  4.0],
              [1.0, 2.0,  6.0]])
x = np.array([0.4, -1.1, 0.8])

def quad(v):
    return v @ A @ v                 # x^T A x, a scalar

grad_correct = (A + A.T) @ x         # the honest identity, eq-grad-quad
grad_naive   = 2 * A @ x             # the common mistake (only valid if A symmetric)

# finite-difference truth
def fd_grad(fn, v, h=1e-6):
    g = np.zeros_like(v)
    for j in range(len(v)):
        e = np.zeros_like(v); e[j] = h
        g[j] = (fn(v + e) - fn(v - e)) / (2 * h)
    return g

grad_true = fd_grad(quad, x)
print("finite-difference (truth)  :", np.round(grad_true, 6))
print("(A + A^T) x   [correct]     :", np.round(grad_correct, 6))
print("2 A x         [naive]       :", np.round(grad_naive, 6))
print("error of naive 2Ax          :", np.round(grad_naive - grad_true, 6))
```

The finite-difference truth matches $(A + A^\top)x$ exactly and the naive $2Ax$ is wrong by
$(A - A^\top)x$ — the contribution of the antisymmetric part, exactly as the interview-box
analysis predicted. This is the honest boundary of the convenient $2Ax$ shortcut: it is a
*special case*, valid only for symmetric $A$, and an asymmetric $A$ breaks it silently (the
formula returns a plausible-looking vector that is simply incorrect). In practice the matrices we
differentiate — $X^\top X$, covariance matrices, Hessians — are symmetric by construction
([GEOM-16]), so $2Ax$ is what you will use most; but knowing *why* it holds keeps you from
misapplying it.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The mechanics of this article are robust — partial differentiation is just Calc 1, and the
identities are exact — but there are honest places it strains or is the wrong frame.

## Non-differentiable losses: the gradient does not exist {#sec-nondiff}

Everything here assumes the partials exist. Several losses central to modern data science are
*not* differentiable everywhere. The absolute-value loss $|r|$ (least absolute deviations,
robust regression) has a corner at $r = 0$; the hinge loss of an SVM and the ReLU activation
$\max(0, z)$ have kinks; the $\ell_1$ penalty $\|\beta\|_1$ that drives the lasso has corners
along every axis. At those kinks @def-partial's limit does not exist — the slope from the left
differs from the slope on the right — and the gradient is genuinely undefined. @fig-kink shows
the offending point.

```{python}
#| label: fig-kink
#| fig-cap: 'Where the gradient fails to exist. The absolute-value function $|r|$ (left) and ReLU $\\max(0,r)$ (right) both have a corner at $r=0$: the left-hand and right-hand slopes disagree, so the Calc-1 limit defining the derivative does not exist there. Smooth differentiation has nothing to say at the kink — the correct tool is the *subgradient*, the set of all slopes that lie below the function (shaded fan), which Optimization develops for exactly these losses.'
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.6))
r = np.linspace(-2, 2, 200)

axL.plot(r, np.abs(r), color=VEC, lw=2.4)
# fan of subgradient slopes at 0
for s in np.linspace(-1, 1, 9):
    axL.plot(r, s * r, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.5)
axL.scatter([0], [0], color=GRAD, s=50, zorder=5)
axL.set_title(r"$|r|$: corner at $r=0$"); axL.set_xlabel("$r$"); axL.set_ylim(-0.5, 2)

axR.plot(r, np.maximum(0, r), color=VEC, lw=2.4)
for s in np.linspace(0, 1, 7):
    axR.plot(r, s * r, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.5)
axR.scatter([0], [0], color=GRAD, s=50, zorder=5)
axR.set_title(r"ReLU $\max(0,r)$: kink at $r=0$"); axR.set_xlabel("$r$"); axR.set_ylim(-0.5, 2)
plt.tight_layout()
```

**What you reach for instead:** the **subgradient** — the set of slopes of all lines that touch
the function from below at the kink — which generalizes the gradient to convex non-smooth
functions and is the object subgradient descent and proximal methods use. That machinery belongs
to the Optimization track; here we only flag that the smooth calculus stops at the corner and
name its successor.

## The curse of layout, and when indices beat cleverness {#sec-layout-failure}

The second failure mode is human, not mathematical: complicated expressions (think a derivative
through a softmax inside a cross-entropy, or $\partial/\partial X$ of $\mathrm{tr}(AXBX^\top)$)
invite layout slips, and a single dropped transpose silently corrupts the answer. The honest
advice is the one from the Pitfall box: **when an expression gets hairy, abandon the identities
and drop to indices** (@sec-micro). Computing $\partial f/\partial x_j$ one component at a time
is slower but has *no convention to get wrong* — the indices force the correct shape. The clean
identities are for the cases you have already verified; the index computation is the ground truth
you fall back to. And in production, the autodiff engine inside every deep-learning framework is
doing exactly the chain rule of @sec-chain, mechanically and without layout errors — which is why
hand-derivation is, in practice, a tool for *understanding and checking*, not for grinding out
every gradient by hand.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When would you actually differentiate by hand versus let autodiff do it?"* By hand: to
*understand* a method, to *derive a closed form* (the normal equations, a Newton step), to
*sanity-check* an autodiff result against a known formula, and in interviews. By machine: anytime
the computation graph is large or changing — neural networks, complex pipelines — where autodiff
applies the same chain rule @eq-chain node by node, exactly and tirelessly. The skills are
complementary: autodiff frees you from the bookkeeping, but you cannot debug a wrong gradient, a
vanishing gradient, or a numerically unstable one without the hand-calculus this article builds.
The finite-difference check in the examples is the bridge — it is how you confirm an analytic
gradient (yours or the framework's) is correct.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from the matrix and decomposition machinery of Movements III–V — in particular the
**dot product and norm** ([GEOM-04]), **matrices as linear maps** ([GEOM-08]), and the
**transpose and symmetry** ([GEOM-10]) — and you have now lifted calculus above the Calc-1 floor
for the only time in the track, building the partial derivative, the gradient, the Jacobian, and
the chain rule from scratch. From here Movement VI continues:

- **[GEOM-23] — The Gradient as a Direction in Space.** We computed the gradient *vector* here;
  next we prove it points in the direction of steepest ascent and is perpendicular to the level
  sets, cashing in the dot-product geometry of [GEOM-04]. The pictures in @fig-gradients and
  @fig-ls-descent are stated there as theorems.
- **[GEOM-24] — The Hessian and the Curvature of a Surface.** Differentiate the gradient *again*
  to get the matrix of second partials; its definiteness ([GEOM-18]) decides whether a critical
  point $\nabla f = 0$ is a minimum, maximum, or saddle.
- **[GEOM-25] — The Gradient and Hessian of a Quadratic Form.** The convergence point: applying
  @eq-grad-quad and @eq-grad-quad-sym to $f(x) = \tfrac12 x^\top A x - b^\top x$ gives $\nabla f =
  Ax - b$, so minimizing a quadratic *is* solving $Ax = b$ — and the least-squares gradient we
  derived in @sec-chain is finished there in full.

Downstream of the track, this article **discharges [INFR-B2]'s** multivariable critical-point
condition (the score equation $\nabla \ell(\theta) = 0$) and supplies the **gradient object every
Optimization algorithm consumes** — gradient descent, Newton's method, and the rest apply this
calculus, and link back here rather than re-deriving it.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The reference that ends layout arguments.** K. B. Petersen and M. S. Pedersen, *The Matrix
  Cookbook* — a compendium of matrix-derivative identities. Use it as a lookup table *after* you
  have internalized the index method of @sec-micro, never as a substitute for it; it states
  results in a fixed layout and a mismatched convention is the usual source of a wrong transpose.
- **The honest treatment of non-smoothness.** The subgradient and proximal calculus that handle
  the kinks of @sec-failure are developed in the Optimization track; the classic source is
  Rockafellar, *Convex Analysis* (1970), for the subdifferential of a convex function.
- **Automatic differentiation.** The chain rule of @sec-chain, applied mechanically over a
  computation graph, is reverse-mode autodiff (backpropagation). Baydin et al., "Automatic
  Differentiation in Machine Learning: a Survey" (*JMLR*, 2018), is the clean modern overview;
  every framework's gradient is @eq-chain repeated node by node.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *Why does the gradient point steepest
  uphill?* — the geometry of [GEOM-23]. *What does the second derivative tell us?* — the Hessian
  of [GEOM-24]. *What is the gradient of a determinant or a matrix inverse?* — the matrix-calculus
  cases of @sec-matrix-deriv, which lean on [GEOM-13] and recur in Gaussian-likelihood work in the
  Inference track.
